event: Hunter-gatherers begin erecting the monumental stone enclosures of Göbekli Tepe, among the world's oldest known temple complexes.
The large Neolithic settlement of Çatalhöyük flourishes in central Anatolia, marking early urban and agricultural life.
Assyrian merchants establish trading colonies (karum) at Kültepe-Kanesh, leaving Anatolia's earliest written records.
The Hittites consolidate a powerful kingdom centered at Hattusa, dominating much of Anatolia.
The Hittites and Egyptians fight the Battle of Kadesh, later sealed by one of history's earliest known peace treaties.
Greek-speaking settlers begin colonizing the Aegean coast of Anatolia, founding Ionian cities such as Miletus and Ephesus.
The Hittite Empire collapses amid the wider Bronze Age upheavals and Sea Peoples migrations.
The kingdom of Phrygia rises in west-central Anatolia, traditionally associated with King Midas.
King Croesus rules the wealthy kingdom of Lydia, credited with minting some of the first true coins.
Cyrus the Great of Persia conquers Lydia, bringing Anatolia under Achaemenid rule.
Alexander the Great defeats the Persians at the Granicus River, beginning Hellenistic domination of Anatolia.
The kingdom of Pergamon is bequeathed to Rome, forming the core of the Roman province of Asia.
Pompey annexes the remaining Anatolian kingdoms, completing Roman control over Asia Minor.
Emperor Constantine refounds Byzantium as Constantinople, making it the capital of the Roman world.
Emperor Justinian completes the great church of Hagia Sophia in Constantinople.
The Seljuk Turks defeat the Byzantines at the Battle of Manzikert, opening Anatolia to Turkish settlement.
The Sultanate of Rum is established, anchoring Turkish rule in Anatolia with its capital at Konya.
The Mongols defeat the Seljuks at Köse Dağ, fragmenting Anatolia into rival Turkish beyliks.
Osman I founds the Ottoman beylik in northwestern Anatolia, origin of the Ottoman Empire.
The Ottomans capture Bursa, which becomes their first major capital.
The Ottomans defeat a Balkan coalition at the Battle of Kosovo, extending their power into Europe.
Timur defeats and captures Sultan Bayezid I at the Battle of Ankara, triggering an Ottoman interregnum.
Mehmed II conquers Constantinople, ending the Byzantine Empire and making the city the Ottoman capital.
Selim I conquers Egypt and the Levant, and the Ottomans assume guardianship of Islam's holy cities.
Suleiman the Magnificent besieges Vienna, marking the Ottoman Empire's furthest advance into Central Europe.
A Christian coalition defeats the Ottoman fleet at the Battle of Lepanto, checking Ottoman naval supremacy.
The failed second siege of Vienna begins the long Ottoman retreat from Central Europe.
The Treaty of Karlowitz forces major territorial losses, signaling Ottoman decline in Europe.
The Tanzimat reforms begin, modernizing Ottoman administration, law, and the military.
The first Ottoman constitution is promulgated, briefly establishing a parliament.
The Young Turk Revolution restores the constitution and ushers in a new political era.
Ottoman forces repel the Allied invasion at Gallipoli, while the deportations and massacres of Armenians take place.
The Grand National Assembly convenes in Ankara under Mustafa Kemal as the Turkish War of Independence intensifies.
The Ottoman sultanate is abolished, ending more than six centuries of dynastic rule.
The Treaty of Lausanne secures international recognition, and the Republic of Turkey is proclaimed with Atatürk as president.
The caliphate is abolished and a secular republican constitution is adopted.
Turkey adopts the Latin-based alphabet, replacing Arabic script as part of sweeping reforms.
Women gain full voting rights and the right to stand for parliament.
Mustafa Kemal Atatürk, founder of the republic, dies and is succeeded by İsmet İnönü.
Turkey joins the United Nations as a founding member after declaring war on the Axis.
The Democrat Party wins Turkey's first fully free multiparty elections.
Turkey joins NATO, firmly aligning with the Western bloc during the Cold War.
A military coup overthrows the government, beginning a recurring pattern of army intervention.
Turkey invades northern Cyprus following a Greek-backed coup on the island.
A military coup imposes martial law amid widespread political violence.
The Kurdistan Workers' Party (PKK) launches an armed insurgency in the southeast.
A devastating earthquake near İzmit kills more than 17,000 people.
Turkey formally opens accession negotiations to join the European Union.
A failed military coup attempt triggers a sweeping state of emergency and purges.
A referendum approves a shift to an executive presidential system, expanding presidential powers.
Catastrophic earthquakes in the south kill over 50,000 people as Turkey marks its republic's centenary.
